---
title: "4.6 Vec and slices"
---

## Explanation

`Vec<T>` owns a contiguous 1D buffer of values. For owned 1D numerical data in
this course, `Vec<f64>` is the standard storage type.

A slice borrows part or all of a buffer. Use `&[T]` for a read-only borrow and
`&mut [T]` for a mutable borrow. Function boundaries should usually take slices
instead of owned vectors when the function only needs to read or change
existing data.

```rust
fn mean(xs: &[f64]) -> Option<f64> {
    if xs.is_empty() {
        None
    } else {
        Some(xs.iter().sum::<f64>() / xs.len() as f64)
    }
}

fn scale_in_place(xs: &mut [f64], alpha: f64) {
    for x in xs {
        *x *= alpha;
    }
}
```

This interface works for a full vector, a part of a vector, or other 1D storage
that can be borrowed as a slice.

## Things to look up

- `Vec<T>`
- Slice
- `&[T]`
- `&mut [T]`
- Contiguous memory

## Exercise

Create tests for `mean` and `scale_in_place`. Include a non-empty slice, an
empty slice for `mean`, and a small vector whose values are changed by
`scale_in_place`.

## Notes for the exercise

- Do not take `&Vec<f64>` when `&[f64]` is enough.
- Use `Vec<f64>` for owned 1D numerical storage.
- Check both returned values and in-place mutation.
